answer: Apply firm, direct pressure with any clean cloth. Hold continuously — do not lift to check. If cloth soaks through, add more on top without removing the first. Keep pressure until help arrives.

Check for breathing — look for chest rise, listen and feel for breath. If breathing: recovery position on their side, top knee bent forward. If not breathing and you know CPR, begin immediately. Call 112 if not already done.
Keep them still and warm. Talk to them even if unconscious — reassurance helps. Do not give food or water. Send someone to the road junction to flag down the ambulance.
Do NOT remove the helmet. Incorrect removal can worsen a spinal injury. Only remove if the airway is completely blocked and you cannot open it any other way. Wait for trained responders.

Read your GPS coordinates from the map. Look for road signs, kilometre markers, or landmark names nearby. Share the Google Maps link from the Share button. Tell the operator the direction you are travelling.

Under Section 134A of India's Motor Vehicles Act 2019, you are legally protected from civil and criminal liability for helping in good faith. You cannot be detained or forced to disclose personal information.
Move everyone at least 50 metres away immediately. Do not re-enter the vehicle. Call 101 for fire and 112 for all services. Stay upwind of the smoke.
Dial 112. This number connects to emergency services in most countries worldwide and redirects to the local dispatcher. It works even without a SIM card.
Do not try to straighten the limb. Support the injury with clothing or padding above and below the fracture. Keep the person still and calm until help arrives.
The nearest hospital is shown on the map. Tap its card to call directly. If transporting the victim yourself, call the hospital first so they prepare.

If trained: 30 chest compressions (hard and fast, 2 per second) then 2 rescue breaths. If untrained: compression-only CPR — just push hard and fast in the centre of the chest. Do not stop until help arrives.
Do not move a person with a suspected spinal injury unless there is immediate danger (fire, traffic). If you must move them, support the head and neck in a straight line and move as a unit.
Keep them lying down with legs slightly elevated if no spinal injury is suspected. Keep them warm with a blanket or clothing. Do not give food or water. Talk to them and monitor breathing.
If conscious: give up to 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades. Then up to 5 abdominal thrusts. Alternate until airway clears or they become unconscious. If unconscious, call 112 and begin CPR.
Cool the burn with cool (not ice cold) running water for at least 10 minutes. Do not apply butter, oil, or toothpaste. Cover loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth. Call 112 for serious burns.
If breathing is laboured, loosen any tight clothing around the neck and chest. Keep them in a position they find most comfortable to breathe. Call 112 immediately.
For children: use gentle pressure for bleeding. CPR uses 2 fingers for infants, 1-2 hands for small children. Use proportional force. Keep them warm and calm. Call 112 immediately.

Do not place a pregnant person flat on their back — tilt them 15-30 degrees to the left side to relieve pressure on the main blood vessel. Keep them warm and calm. Call 112 immediately and inform about pregnancy.

Triage: quickly check who is breathing without help, who needs help to breathe, and who is not breathing. Help those who are breathing and responsive first. Send someone to flag down help on the road.
Secure the scene first. Turn on hazard lights of any driveable vehicle. Place warning triangles 50m back if available. Keep bystanders off the road. Only then provide first aid.
Dial 112 for all emergencies in India. 108 for ambulance, 100 for police, 101 for fire. 112 works even without a SIM card.
If the person is conscious and can swallow: give sugar (juice, glucose, sweet biscuit). If unconscious: do not give anything by mouth. Place in recovery position and call 112.

Do not restrain or hold them down. Clear the area of hard objects. Place something soft under their head. Time the seizure. After it stops, place them in recovery position. Call 112 if seizure lasts more than 5 minutes.
Keep them still and reassure them. Watch for vomiting, confusion, unequal pupils, or loss of consciousness — these indicate serious injury. Do not give pain medication. Call 112 immediately.
Remove from water safely without endangering yourself. If not breathing, start CPR immediately. Even if they seem fine, all drowning victims need hospital assessment. Call 112.
Flush the eye immediately with clean water for at least 15 minutes. Do not rub the eye. Cover loosely with a clean cloth. Call 112 or go to nearest eye hospital immediately.
Do not induce vomiting unless told by medical professionals. Keep the person calm and still. Bring the container or identify the substance. Call 112 or poison control.

Sit them down in a comfortable position. Loosen tight clothing. If they have their own nitrate spray or tablets, assist them in using it. Do not give aspirin unless they are conscious and not allergic. Call 112.
Remember FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 112. Do not give food or water. Lay them on their stronger side. Note the time symptoms started.
If they have an EpiPen, help them use it (outer thigh). Lay them flat with legs raised unless breathing is difficult. Call 112 immediately. Anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes.
Do not try to pull a trapped person free — this risks crush syndrome. Keep them talking, warm, and still. Call 112 and tell them the person is trapped — rescue teams have specialised equipment.
Do NOT touch the person while they are still in contact with the electricity source. Switch off the power first. Only when it is safe, begin first aid and call 112.
Move them to a warm shelter. Remove wet clothing. Wrap in blankets starting from the core. Give warm (not hot) drinks if fully conscious. Call 112 for moderate/severe cases.
Move them to shade. Remove excess clothing. Fan and spray with cool water. Apply ice packs to armpits, neck, and groin. Give water if conscious. Call 112 — heat stroke is an emergency.
Lean forward slightly (not back). Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10-15 minutes. Breathe through the mouth. Do not pack the nose. If bleeding doesn't stop, go to hospital.

Apply firm pressure to the stump. Wrap the severed part in a clean wet cloth, place in a sealed bag, then place that bag in cold water (not ice). Bring the part to hospital — reimplantation may be possible.
Do NOT remove the object — it may be preventing blood loss. Stabilise it with padding around it. Keep the person still. Call 112 immediately.
Check for immediate dangers (fire, fuel leak). Turn off ignition. Call 112. Keep victims still unless in immediate danger. Use hazard lights and warning devices.
Do not remove helmet. Check for breathing around the helmet visor. Control any bleeding. Keep them still for spinal injury protection. Call 112.
Do not move them — assume spinal injury. Control bleeding with pressure. Keep them warm. Reassure them. Mark the area to prevent further vehicles. Call 112.
Keep them still and calm. Do not give any medication unless specifically prescribed and available. Reassure them that help is coming. Monitor breathing and consciousness. Call 112.
Speak in a calm, clear voice. Stay with them. Slow, deep breaths together. Tell them their name if you know it. Reassure them that help is on the way. Physical contact (hand holding) helps.
When you call 112, an operator will answer. Tell them: your location (GPS coordinates or landmarks), how many people are injured, the type of emergency. Stay on the line — they will guide you.
On national highways, call 112 and also the highway helpline if posted. The NHAI emergency number is 1033. Flag down passing vehicles for help. Use hazard lights and warning triangles.
Turn on all vehicle hazard lights. Use your phone torch. Signal oncoming traffic by waving the torch. Stay visible. Call 112 and describe any visible landmarks or kilometre markers.

Move to higher ground for better signal. Try calling from different spots. SMS to 112 if voice call fails. Ask local people for the nearest dispensary or PHC. The nearest hospital is shown on the map even offline.
Tell the emergency team the patient's blood group if known. This information should be in the Victim ID card if the person set it up. Blood type is critical if transfusion is needed.
If the patient is a registered organ donor, inform the medical team at hospital. This is noted on their Victim ID card if set up. Medical teams handle this in compliance with THOA 1994.
Elderly patients are at higher risk from blood loss and shock. Keep them warm. Do not move them unnecessarily. Their bones are more fragile — avoid applying excessive pressure. Call 112 immediately.
The app has already sent an alert SMS with your location to your emergency contacts. You can share your location again using the Share Location button in the Emergency panel.

Call 112 first. Then check if the scene is safe for you to enter. Control serious bleeding, maintain airway, keep the victim warm and still. You are legally protected for helping under Section 134A, MV Act.
Do not give any medication unless you are certain of what it is and the patient is conscious and can swallow. Inform emergency responders of all medications the patient may have taken.
Turn the person to their side to prevent choking on vomit. Clear the airway gently. If unconscious, maintain recovery position. Do not give water.
Encourage slow, deep breathing if the person is conscious. Loosen anything tight around the chest. If they have asthma and a rescue inhaler, assist them in using it.
Suspect internal bleeding if: rapid pulse, pale/cold skin, pain without visible wound, distended abdomen. Keep them still, warm, and call 112 immediately. Internal bleeding is life-threatening.
Typical response time on Indian national highways is 25-35 minutes. Keep the person stable and monitor their condition. Update the 112 operator if condition changes.
Support the injured side with a firmly folded cloth or blanket. Have them lean slightly toward the injured side. Do not bind the chest tightly. Call 112.

For snake bite: immobilise the bitten limb below heart level, keep the person still and calm, do not suck the venom or apply tourniquet. Call 112 immediately. For dog bite: clean with soap and water for 15 minutes, seek rabies prophylaxis.
Remove contact lenses if the person is unconscious or if their eyes are injured. Leave glasses on unless they interfere with treatment or the patient is unconscious.
Collect and secure any ID documents near the victim but do not search their personal belongings. Hand these to police or medical responders when they arrive.
If safe, take photos of the scene for insurance and police records before moving vehicles. Note the time, location, and details of vehicles involved. This is not obstructing traffic — it is evidence.
File an FIR at the nearest police station within 24 hours for insurance claims. The hospital will typically issue a medico-legal case (MLC) report. Keep all receipts for treatment.
Use whatever clean material is available — a shirt, cloth, or scarf can work as a bandage. The goal is to apply pressure to wounds and keep the person stable until professional help arrives.
Do not move an accident victim unless there is immediate danger (fire, sinking, oncoming traffic). Moving incorrectly can worsen spinal injuries. Wait for trained responders if possible.
Start CPR immediately if there is no breathing or pulse. Do not assume death. Continue until ambulance arrives or you are physically unable. The emergency operator can guide you.
Your immediate focus is saving life. Determining fault is a matter for police and courts later. You are legally protected for emergency assistance under Section 134A, MV Act 2019.

AEDs are available at some Indian airports, malls, and corporate buildings. If one is nearby, send someone to get it. Turn it on and follow its audio instructions exactly — it will not shock a person who does not need it.
Give police a brief account of events. You cannot be detained if you were helping as a Good Samaritan under Section 134A, MV Act. Your voluntary statement can be given later at the station.
Tap any hospital marker on the map for name, distance, and phone number. The nearest trauma-capable hospital is listed first. Call ahead so they can prepare on arrival.
Check pulse at the wrist (radial) or neck (carotid). Normal adult pulse: 60-100 per minute. Check breathing by observing chest rise. Note skin colour — pale/blue indicates poor circulation.
108 is India's ambulance emergency number. When you call, tell them your GPS location, number of victims, and type of injuries. They will dispatch the nearest available ambulance.
Calling ahead to the hospital allows them to activate the trauma team, prepare blood, and clear a bay. Give them: number of patients, type of injuries, ETA, and patient's blood group if known.
Check the map — hospital distances are shown on each card in km. Driving time depends on traffic. For critical patients, ambulance transport is safer than private vehicle.

If the victim has the RoadSoS app, their emergency contacts have already been notified by SMS. Otherwise, check their phone for an ICE (In Case of Emergency) contact or look in their wallet for an emergency card.
The map shows hospitals even offline from bundled data. If no markers appear, tap the hospital category chip to make sure it's enabled. Check the Nearby list for a text listing.
Tap a hospital card and press Navigate to open Google Maps with directions. You can also use the coordinates from this app in any map app.
If the victim has RoadSoS, their Victim ID card is accessible from the lock screen notification. Tap 'Medical Card' on the notification to view their blood group, conditions, and allergies.
The Victim ID QR code contains the patient's blood group, allergies, conditions, and emergency contact. Any QR scanner app can read it. It is accessible from the lock screen without a PIN.
Use the Share Location button in the Emergency panel to send a pre-formatted message with your GPS coordinates, Google Maps link, and nearest hospital details via any messaging app.
Do NOT give water or food to an accident victim. They may require surgery and must have an empty stomach. Reassure them that help is coming.
Providing comfort is important. If the person is conscious and wishes to pray, support them. Focus on keeping them calm and breathing steadily until help arrives.

Section 134A of the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act 2019 grants complete legal protection to anyone who voluntarily helps a road accident victim in good faith. You cannot be detained, forced to give personal details, or held liable for any outcome.

RoadSoS is a road accident emergency app. It shows nearby hospitals, allows one-tap emergency calls, detects crashes automatically, and gives bystander first aid guidance. Your location and emergency contacts have already been alerted.
